note: Hard NDA-claim cases for verifier calibration (the NDA counterpart to cases.json). `gold` is the intended verdict (a defensible human judgment under STRICT grounding: judge ONLY the NDA snippet text). Covers the six NDA areas — definition of Confidential Information, exclusions, permitted use, obligations/standard of care, term/duration, and return/destruction. The labeling tool collects YOUR verdict as the oracle; gold is for QA/compare only.

cases.contract: 1. "Confidential Information" means all non-public information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, whether oral, written, or electronic, that is marked confidential or that a reasonable person would understand to be confidential given the nature of the information.
cases.claim: Orally disclosed information can qualify as Confidential Information.
cases.why_hard: clean restatement — the definition expressly lists 'oral'

cases.contract: 1. "Confidential Information" means non-public information disclosed in writing and marked "Confidential" at the time of disclosure.
cases.claim: Information disclosed orally is covered as Confidential Information.
cases.why_hard: this definition is writing-only + marked; oral is excluded — contradicts a plausible default

cases.contract: 1. "Confidential Information" means information disclosed by the Disclosing Party that is marked "Confidential" or, if disclosed orally, identified as confidential at disclosure and summarized in writing within thirty (30) days.
cases.claim: Orally disclosed information is confidential only if it is summarized in writing within 30 days.
cases.why_hard: requires reading the oral-disclosure follow-up condition precisely

cases.contract: 1. "Confidential Information" means information marked "Confidential" or, if oral, reduced to writing within fifteen (15) days.
cases.claim: Oral disclosures must be confirmed in writing within 30 days to be confidential.
cases.why_hard: subtle number error: 15 vs 30 days — looks right, isn't

cases.contract: 2. The obligations herein do not apply to information that (a) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of the Receiving Party, (b) was rightfully known to the Receiving Party before disclosure, or (c) is independently developed without use of the Confidential Information.
cases.claim: Information the Receiving Party already lawfully knew before disclosure is excluded from confidentiality obligations.
cases.why_hard: faithful restatement of exclusion (b)

cases.contract: 2. The obligations do not apply to information that is or becomes publicly available through no fault of the Receiving Party.
cases.claim: Publicly available information is excluded from the confidentiality obligations.
cases.why_hard: omits the load-bearing qualifier 'through no fault of the receiving party'

cases.contract: 2. The obligations do not apply to information independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to or use of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information.
cases.claim: Information independently developed using the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information is still excluded from the obligations.
cases.why_hard: directly contradicted: the carve-out requires 'without use of' the Confidential Information

cases.contract: 2. The exclusions do not apply to information that (a) is publicly available, (b) was already known, (c) is independently developed, or (d) is received from a third party without breach of any obligation of confidentiality.
cases.claim: Information received from a third party who was free to disclose it is excluded from the obligations.
cases.why_hard: exclusion (d) — 'without breach of any obligation' = third party free to disclose

cases.contract: 3. The Receiving Party shall use the Confidential Information solely to evaluate a potential business relationship between the parties (the "Purpose").
cases.claim: The Receiving Party may use the Confidential Information to develop competing products.
cases.why_hard: 'solely to evaluate ... the Purpose' forbids competing-product use; tempting real-world inference the text forbids

cases.contract: 3. The Receiving Party shall use the Confidential Information solely for the Purpose of evaluating the proposed transaction and for no other purpose.
cases.claim: Use of the Confidential Information is limited to evaluating the proposed transaction.
cases.why_hard: faithful restatement of the 'solely for the Purpose' limitation

cases.contract: 3. The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information to its employees and advisors who have a need to know for the Purpose and who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as protective as those herein.
cases.claim: The Receiving Party may freely share the Confidential Information with any of its employees.
cases.why_hard: 'freely ... any' overstates: limited to need-to-know employees bound by equivalent obligations

cases.contract: 3. The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information to its employees and contractors who have a need to know, provided they are bound by written confidentiality obligations at least as protective as this Agreement.
cases.claim: The Receiving Party may share Confidential Information with need-to-know employees if those employees are under equivalent confidentiality obligations.
cases.why_hard: accurate paraphrase of a multi-condition permitted-disclosure clause

cases.contract: 4. The Receiving Party shall protect the Confidential Information using the same degree of care it uses for its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care.
cases.claim: The Receiving Party must protect the Confidential Information with at least a reasonable degree of care.
cases.why_hard: the 'but in no event less than reasonable' floor is explicit

cases.contract: 4. The Receiving Party shall protect the Confidential Information using the same degree of care it uses to protect its own confidential information of like importance.
cases.claim: The Receiving Party must use the highest degree of care, equivalent to that of a fiduciary, to protect the Confidential Information.
cases.why_hard: 'same degree of care it uses for its own' is not a fiduciary/highest standard — invented heightened duty

cases.contract: 4. If the Receiving Party is compelled by law or court order to disclose Confidential Information, it shall give the Disclosing Party prompt written notice, where legally permitted, so the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order.
cases.claim: If legally compelled to disclose, the Receiving Party must notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing, where the law permits such notice.
cases.why_hard: requires reading the compelled-disclosure carve-out with its 'where legally permitted' qualifier

cases.contract: 4. The Receiving Party shall not reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any prototypes or software disclosed as Confidential Information.
cases.claim: The Receiving Party may not reverse engineer anything the Disclosing Party shares.
cases.why_hard: the bar covers prototypes/software specifically, not 'anything shared' — 'anything' overstates

cases.contract: 5. This Agreement shall remain in effect for two (2) years from the Effective Date. The confidentiality obligations shall survive for three (3) years after each disclosure.
cases.claim: The confidentiality obligations last for two years from the Effective Date.
cases.why_hard: conflates the 2-year agreement term with the 3-year survival of the obligations — a material number/scope error

cases.contract: 5. The confidentiality obligations shall survive for five (5) years following the termination or expiration of this Agreement.
cases.claim: The confidentiality obligations continue for five years after the Agreement ends.
cases.why_hard: clean restatement of the survival period

cases.contract: 5. With respect to any Confidential Information that constitutes a trade secret, the obligations of confidentiality shall continue for as long as such information remains a trade secret under applicable law.
cases.claim: Confidentiality obligations for trade secrets expire after five years.
cases.why_hard: invents a 5-year cap; the text ties trade-secret protection to trade-secret status, with no fixed term

cases.contract: 6. Upon the Disclosing Party's written request or upon termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all Confidential Information, including all copies, and certify such destruction in writing.
cases.claim: On request, the Receiving Party must return all copies of the Confidential Information.
cases.why_hard: the clause is return-OR-destroy; 'must return' picks one of two permitted options as if mandatory

cases.contract: 6. Upon termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy all Confidential Information; provided, however, that the Receiving Party may retain one archival copy solely for legal-compliance purposes, subject to continuing confidentiality.
cases.claim: The Receiving Party must destroy every copy of the Confidential Information and may keep none.
cases.why_hard: ignores the archival-copy proviso; 'keep none' contradicts the retained-copy carve-out

cases.contract: 7. Nothing in this Agreement grants the Receiving Party any license or right in the Confidential Information except the limited right to use it for the Purpose. No agency, partnership, or joint venture is created.
cases.claim: This Agreement grants the Receiving Party a license to commercialize the Disclosing Party's intellectual property.
cases.why_hard: expressly disclaimed: only a limited use-for-the-Purpose right, no commercialization license
